# Slop Audit

The Slop Audit is an open standard for measuring software quality, stewarded by the Open Honest Foundation and released under Apache-2.0. It is the common name for the standard formally designated the Honest Audit. Author: Adam Zachary Wasserman.

## Key message

Any organization that must attest to the correctness of its software (regulated industries, banks, insurers, pharmaceutical and medical-device manufacturers, public infrastructure) needs to understand that conventional code coverage (line coverage) cannot guarantee that every permutation has been tested, no matter what an IT department reports. Whether exhaustive behavioural verification is even possible is a structural property of the code, not of the test suite: when mutable state is unbounded, the reachable-state space explodes past anything a test suite can cover. The Slop Audit measures that structure directly (mutable-state ratio, decision-space coverage, test determinism), so an attestation of correctness can rest on evidence rather than on a coverage figure that was never designed to carry that weight. The first cross-language measurement on 200 public codebases found approximately 99% of non-React enterprise codebases structurally incapable of exhaustive behavioural verification.

## What it is

The Slop Audit replaces the impression of "slop" with a measurement: an evidence-based, reproducible score of any codebase against the published thresholds of named compliance frameworks, across 18 dimensions and four layers of evidence. A polished codebase can score badly and rough-looking code can score well; only the structure decides. It is independent of the Honest Framework and applies to any codebase in any language.

## The four-layer model

Phase 0 runs Layers 1 through 3 (reproducible by trained assessors). Phase 1 adds Layer 4 (elite architectural judgment, transferred by apprenticeship).

- Layer 1, quantitative git-history assessment: mechanical, 20 numerical indicators from git history and static analysis (mutable-state ratio, decision-space coverage, test determinism, delete/add ratio, secret scan, type-escape density, fuzzy duplication, god-file concentration, and others).
- Layer 2, quantitative artifact assessment: mechanical per-dimension inspection scored Present, Partial, Absent, or Not Applicable, with cited file paths and line numbers.
- Layer 3, qualitative specified judgment: three to five specified markers per dimension with inter-rater-reliability targets.
- Layer 4, architectural synthesis: elite judgment flagged for Phase 1 follow-up, not scored in Phase 0.

A Phase 0 audit of a 50,000 to 250,000 line codebase runs in about five days and yields a Slop Report plus a SOC 2 deliverable a CIO can hand to their SOC 2 auditor.

## The 18 dimensions

4.1 Entitlement system, 4.2 Authentication, 4.3 Inter-service security, 4.4 Multi-tenancy, 4.5 Audit infrastructure, 4.6 Rate limiting, 4.7 Configuration and secrets, 4.8 Caching, 4.9 Notifications, 4.10 CI/CD, 4.11 Containerization, 4.12 Dependency injection, 4.13 Pattern sophistication, 4.14 Architectural philosophy, 4.15 Live documentation, 4.16 SDLC with AI safeguards, 4.17 Tech debt management, 4.18 UX from code.

## Compliance frameworks

Indicators and dimensions are calibrated against SOC 2 Trust Services Criteria, NIST SP 800-53, OSFI B-13, OWASP ASVS, ISO/IEC 25010, WCAG 2.2, Section 508, EN 301 549, AODA, and Quebec Law 25.

## The finding

The first cross-language measurement of the mutable-state ratio (Layer 1) on 200 public open-source codebases finds that approximately 99% of non-React enterprise codebases are structurally incapable of exhaustive behavioural verification.

## Research and status

- OSF DBSYG block (construct and predictive validity), DOI 10.17605/OSF.IO/DBSYG
- Independent validation at UQAM/LATECE (assessor protocols and inter-rater-reliability calibration in preparation)
- Methodology complete, approximately 45,000 words across 18 dimensions, four layers, and the SOC 2 deliverable extraction
- Reference implementation and full specification coming soon under the Open Honest organization on GitHub

## Umbra

Umbra is the newest instrument in the Slop Audit, and the smallest: where the Slop Audit measures whether an entire codebase is structurally capable of exhaustive verification, Umbra applies the same premise to a single module and its test suite. It is an apophatic code auditor: it reports what a test suite is structurally unable to see and proves each gap with a real failing test. Its metric is the Silence index (100 x closeable silence sites / total closeable facets), the honest complement to coverage: higher means more blind spots. It audits Python, TypeScript, JavaScript, Java, Rust, and Go, each parsed with tree-sitter and run with its own native test runner and coverage tool. A deterministic layer runs with zero model calls; GPT-5.6 is used only where structure runs out, on one isolated function plus its type contract plus one located gap, and every generated test is executed and kept only if it genuinely fails. Untyped code is reported as 100% silence, closeable by declaring a type rather than writing a test. Umbra runs as a plugin inside the coding agent's loop and as a standalone CLI. Open source under Apache-2.0 at https://github.com/openhonest/umbra. Page: https://slopaudit.org/umbra.html.

## Links
